Fnrn Polrrrcel PRACrrcEs C ouurssroN
428 J Street . Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA gigl4-232g
(9t6) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322_0886
January 5,2010
Prescilla Dugard
City of Murrieta
Stutz Artiano Shinoff & Holtz
2488 Historic Decatur Road, Suite 200
San Diego, CA 92106-6113
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-10-200
Dear Ms. Dugard:
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Thii letter is based on the facts presented.
The Fair Political Practices Commission ("the Commission") does not act as a finder of fact
when it renders assistance. (ln re oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.) Because- your question is
general in nature, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.2'
Please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore
offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other laws that may apply such as Govemment
Code Section 1090 or common law conflict of interest.
QUESTION
May the City Planner make, participate in making, or influence governmental decisions
legarding an anticipated proposal involving a 1,400 o"." *.u approximately 540 feet from the
City Planner's residence?
I The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 8 I 000 through g I 0 I 4. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fiir political practices
Commission are contained in Sections l8 I l0 through I 8997 of Title 2 of thi Calitbrnia code of Regulations. All
regulatory ref'erences are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.
r Informal assistarce does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or tbrmal
written advice. (Section 83 I t4, Regulation I 8329(cX j).)


File No. I-10-200
Page No. 2
CONCLUSION
The city Planner may take part in the decisions regarding the anticipated proposal so
long as no additional facts establish a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on her
economic interest(s).
F'ACTS
Ln2006, the city^.planning department working with community members prepared a
draft Los Alamos Specific Plan ai an-outgrowth of the city's general plan updur". while the Los
Alamos specific Plan was not adopted iizooa,o.o*-urity lroup ltire ,.cQtvt,,) is currently
attempting to reinvigo^r3le specific planning efforts in the same Los Alamos area. It is
anticipated thar the ceLM will submit a proposal to the city for review.
As anticipated, cQLM's proposal will incorporate a specific plan area of approximately
1'400 acres and designate 168 acres, located on the ixtreme east end of the plan boundaries, for
land use changes increasing the density of residential units per acre. The primary intent of the
proposal is to preserve the balance of the 1,400 acre area foi single-family residential lots at the
culrent density of I unit pet 2.5 acres. The clustering of the new-higher dlnsity lots will also help
to preserve hillsides and other environmentally constrained areas.
Additionall.r, gQt-u's proposal will address water and sewer improvements within the
1,400 acre area and indentify specific programs or mechanisms to establish water and sewer lines
to serve the area including the formation of a community facilities district to help finance the
improvements' currently, this area relies solely on wells for water and septic systems for waste.
within the 1,400 acre area' there is no service irovider for water o. seweifor approximately g00
acres' No water or sewer lines have been installed to date within the remainin! ooo acres despite
the fact that the area is within the boundaries of an existing water/sewer provider.
cQLM's proposal will also evaluate road standards to serve the residents of the area and
address road access for emergency service providers. cunently, there are r"* fuu"a streets
within the 1,400 acre area.
As you describe the effects of CQLM's proposal, there is likely to be ..an incremental
impact on the property values" within thi designated area including ..a minor incremental impact
on the City Plarurer's property," which is her residence and is located approximately 540 feet
from the boundaries of the 1,400 acre area encompassed by the proposal. However, you have
also indicated that any impact on the city Plann"i', prop"iy would be mitigated by the
following facts:
o The proposedland use changes increasing the density for development within the 1,400
acre area are limited to the extreme east end of the area approximately 2 miles for the
City Planner's property.
o The City Planner's property is already connected to the existing water and sewer
infrastructures.


File No. I-10-200
Page No. 3
o The proposed roadways within the 1,400 acre area would not connect to the tract of
property in which the City planner's property is located.
ANALYSIS
Section 87.1.09 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
using his or her official position to influence a govemmental deciiion in whiJh the official has a
financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within
the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect on.9ne or- more of the public official's economic interests. (Section g7103;
Regulation 18700(a).) The commission has adopted an eighrstep standardanalysis for deciding
whether an individual has a disqualifying conflici of intereit in any given govemmental decision.
Step One: Is the individual a,,public official?,,
The Act's conflict-of- interest provisions apply only to "public officials.,, (Sections
87100, 87103; Regulation 18700(bXl).) A "pubtic oiriciai" is'tvery -"-u"r, officer, employee
or consultant of a state or local government agency . . .." (Section aio+t.y The city planner is a
public official within rhe meaning of the Act.
Step Two: Is the official making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental
decision?
A public official_"makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting within the
authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits his or her
agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agieement on behalf of his or her
agency' (Regulation- 18702.1.) A public official "participates in u gor"mmental decision,, when,
acting within the authority of his or her position uno *itt out signif[ant intervening substantive
review, the official negotiates, advises, or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker
regarding the govemmental decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to
use his or her official position to influencJa decision if, for the purpose of influencing, the
official contacts ol appears before any member, officer, employee, tr consultant of his or her
agency' (Regulation 18702.3.) The City Planner is making, participating in making, or
influencing governmental decisions when taking part in citly decisions relarain! celM,s
anticipated proposal.
Step Three: What are the official,s economic interests?
of the economic-interests recognized under the Act3, those interests implicated by your
account of the facts are the following:
3 our analysis is limited to the economic interests you have identitied.


File No. I-10-200
Page No. 4
Real Property -- Apublic official has an economic interest in real property in which he or
she has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Section g7103(b); Regulation 1g703.2.)
Presumably, the City planner has an investment interest of $2,000 or more in her residence.
Thus, she has an economic interest in this property.
Personal Financial Effects -- A public official always has an economic interest in his or
her personal finances- In particular, o gou"*ment decisio, t as a personal financial effect on a
public official if the decision will t"tult in the personal "*p"rr"., income, assets, or liabilities of
the official increasing or decreasing. (Section'sztol; n.g'urution igrd;.f "-'
Step Four: Is the official's economic interest directly or indirecgy involved in the decision?
Regulation 18704.2(a) states that real property in which a public official has an economic
interest is directly involved in a governmentaidelision if any of the following apply:
"( I ) The real property in which the official has an interest, or any part of that
real property, is located within 500 feet of the boundaries (or the proporla
boundaries) of the property which is the subject of the governmental decision . . ..
"(2)The governmental decision involves the zoning or rezoning, annexation
or deannexation, sale, purchase, or lease, or inclusion in oiexclusion fiom any city,
county, district or other local governmental subdivision, of the reat prop"rty in which
the official has an interest or a similar decision affecting trre rear prJf;;.
"(3) The govemmental decision involves the issuance, denial or revocation of
a license, permit or other land use entitlement authorizing a specific ur" o. uses of
the real property in which the official has an interest.
"(4) The governmentar decision involves the imposition, repeal, or
modification of any taxes or fees assessed or imposed on the real pfup.nv in which
the official has an interest.
"(5) The governmental decision is to designate the survey area, to select the
project area, to adopt the preliminary plan, to rorir a project area committee, to
certify the environmentar document, to adopt the redeveiopment ptur, to uaa
territory to the redevelopment area, or to rescind or amend ony of the above
decisions; and real property in which the official has an interest, o, uny p* of it is
located within the boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the redevelop-"n,
area.
a we point out that public officials always have an economic interest in their personal finances. However,
any financial effect the decision may have on the 
-city 
Planner's ..ria"n." is considered an effect on her real
property interest and would not be analyzed separateiy under the "p".ronor financial effects,,ruies. lRegulation
18705'5(a)') Accordingly, the personai financial efrects .ule ooes ;;;;;p.0, to appty to the City ptanner,s
circumstances and we will not discuss it further.


File No. I-10-200
Page No. 5
"(6) The decision involves construction of, or improvements to, streets,
water, sewer, storm drainage or similar facilities, and the real property in which the
official has an interest wilr receive new or improved seryices.,,
Because the city Planner's residence is not located within 500 feet of the property that is
the subject of the governmental decisions and you have not provided any facts indicating that the
govemmental decisions involve any of the typ;s of decisioni outlined i, n.gututio.,
18704'2(a)(2)-(6), it appears that the city Pianner's economic interest in her residence is
indirectlv involved in decisions regarding the ceLM,s anticipateJ;;;;;r.Lt-"
Steps Five and Six: Will there be a reasonably foreseeable material financiat effect on the
official's economic interest?
Materiality
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable impact of a
governmental decision on a public official;s economic interests is material. (Regulation
18700(a)') Different standards apply to determine whether a reasonably foreseeable financial
effect on an economic interest *ili u" material, dependin! on the nature of the economic interest
and whether that interest is directly or indirectiy iruorr"a'in ttre agency,s a""lriorr.
For properties indirectly involved in a governmental decision, Regulation 1g705.2(bxl)
provides that the financial effect is presumed not to be material. Howeve"r, this presumption may
be rebutted by proof that there are specific circumstances regarding the govemmental decision,
its financial effect, and the nature of th" real property in which the public official has an
economic interest that make it reasonably foreseiabie the decision will have a material financial
effect on the real property in which the official has an interest. Examples of these specific
circumstances include, but are not limited to, circumstances where the decision affects:
"(A) Jhe.develo-pment potential or income producing potential of the real
property in which the official has an economic inteiest;
"(B) The use of the real property in which the official has an economic
interest;
"(C) The character of the neighborhood including, but not limited to,
substantial effects on: traffic, view, privacy, intensity of use, noise levels, air
emissions, or similar traits of the neighborirood.,,
Foreseeability
once a public official has determined the materiality standard applicable to each of his or
her economic interests, the next step is determining wtret# it is ,,reason'aury ror.r".able,, that
inuorr.a.l, ,'r{:Jrtil,l,j""ffiUiffi"'omic 
interest is not directlv involved in a governmental decision, ir is indirectly


File No. I-10-200
Page No. 6
the standard will be met. A material financial effect on an economic interest is ..reasonably
foreseeable" if it is substantially likely that one or more of the materiality standards will be met
as a result of the governmental decision. (Regulation 18706(a).) an effect need not be certain to
be considered "reasonably foreseeable," but iimust be more than a mere possibility. (ln re
Thorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.)
Ultimately, whether a material financial effect is foreseeable at the time a decision is
made depends on facts and circumstances peculiar to each case. (In re Thonter, supra, at l9g.)
Because the Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providing advice (ln re Oglesby,
supra, atTl), the foreseeability of a particular financial effect is a dJtermination that must be
left, in most instances, to the informed judgment of the public official.
Nonetheless, we have previously advised that the presumption that an indirectly involved
property will not be materially affected is rebutted only if the gou"--.ntal decision will have a
clearly distinguishable and substantial financial effect tn u, oifi"iol', property. (yangAdvice
Letter, No. I-06-198 and BergerAdvice Letter, No. A-05-054.) In uaopti"g fLgularion
18705'z(b)(l), the Commission noted that "beyond the 500 root range, parTicipation would be
allowed unless something about the decision makes the official', p.o"p"rty .r,i"t out like a sore
thumb."' (commission Minutes of Meeting, october 6,2000, at iage +.)
While you have indicated that there may be "a minor incremental impact on the City
Planner's property," yo-u have also provided that (1) the proposed land use changes are
approximately 2 miles for the City Planner's property, <i> n"City plann.r', frop".ty is already
connected to the existing water and sewer infiastructures, and (:)itre proporJroadways would
not connect to the tract of property in which the City Planner's proprrty is located. From the
facts provided, there is no indication of special circumstances to reLut ihe presumption of non-
materiality. Barring additional facts, it does not appear reasonably foreseeable that decisions
regarding CQLM's anticipated proposal will have a material financial effect on the City
Planner's economic interest in her real property.
Steps Seven and Eight: Does this governmental decision come within any exception to the
confl ict-of-interest rules?
Even if an official has a conflict of interest, disqualification is not required if the
govemmental decision affects the public official's economic interests in a manner that is
indistinguishable from the manner in which the decision will affect the public lenerally. (Section
87 103 ; Regulation 187 07 (a).)
Additionally, in certain rare circumstances, a public official may be called upon to take part
in a decision despite the fact that the official may have a disqualifying conflict of interest under the
"legally required participation" exception. This exception applies only in certain very specific
circumstances where the government agency would u" puruiyr"d from acting. (Section g7l0l;
Regulation 18708.)


File No. I-10-200
Page No. 7
However, you have not presented any facts indicating that the "public generally,, or the
"legally required participation" exceptions are applicable to the City planner,s circumstances, so
we will not address them further.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
By: Brian G. Lau
Counsel, Legal Division

QUESTION: May the City Planner make, participate in making, or influence governmental decisions
legarding an anticipated proposal involving a 1,400 o"." *.u approximately 540 feet from the
City Planner's residence?
r Informal assistarce does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or tbrmal
written advice. (Section 83 I t4, Regulation I 8329(cX j).)

CONCLUSION: The city Planner may take part in the decisions regarding the anticipated proposal so
long as no additional facts establish a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on her
